text.title: Search Intent To Brief Core Rules
text.summary: 5+ testable rules every Search Intent To Brief application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Every brief MUST declare exactly one primary intent class from {Informational, Comparative, Transactional, Navigational}. Multi-intent briefs (e.g. "informational + comparative") MUST be split into two briefs.
rule.rationale: Mixed intent forces the drafter to write two articles in one; both halves underperform. Splitting is cheaper than hedging.

rule.statement: The brief MUST list ≥5 must-cover entities extracted from the union of top-10 SERP titles and headings. Each entity is tagged with `mention_count` (how many top-10 results name it). Drafter MUST cover every entity with `mention_count ≥ 4`.
rule.rationale: Entity coverage is the strongest correlate of topical relevance scoring. Missing high-frequency entities is the single biggest reason AI drafts under-rank.

rule.statement: The brief MUST contain a Jobs-to-be-Done line: "When I <situation>, I want to <motivation>, so I can <outcome>." All three slots filled with concrete strings; no placeholders.
rule.rationale: Without JTBD, the draft addresses the query string, not the reader's underlying problem. JTBD is what converts traffic into engagement.

rule.statement: The brief MUST list every SERP feature observed on the live SERP at brief time: {featured_snippet, people_also_ask, image_pack, video_carousel, knowledge_panel, local_pack, top_stories, sitelinks, none}. For each present feature, the brief MUST declare whether the article will TARGET or IGNORE it.
rule.rationale: SERP features are the highest-CTR real estate. Briefs that ignore them ship articles that rank but get zero clicks.

rule.statement: The brief MUST be written as a contract — required H2s, required word-count range, required internal links, required outbound citations — not as suggestions. A drafter (human or AI) deviating from the contract triggers a rejection at review, not a "looks fine" sign-off.
rule.rationale: Suggestions are ignored under deadline pressure. Contracts force the brief author to do the thinking once and downstream drafters to comply, which is why AI drafting unanchored from a contract produces slop.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
